mappings.rationale: Directly covers functional design and industrial design, the core subject of this section's treatment of the nature and scope of decorative arts and functional design.
Directly covers dress design and dress accessories, explicitly listed under arts of functional design in section C.1.a.
Directly covers typography and printing design, explicitly listed under design of materials for communication in section C.4.b.
Directly relevant to advertising art and design, explicitly listed in section C.4.c.
Relevant to the section's treatment of the design of signs and symbols for identification (section C.4.d) and advertising design (section C.4.c).
Relevant to the section's treatment of interior design as integrated with architecture (section C.3.b), including interior architectural elements.
Relevant to the section's treatment of gems as decorative materials (section B.3) and the crystalline structure of decorative stones.
Covers the properties of materials including clay, glass, metals, wood, and plastics — materials explicitly listed as decorative art media throughout section B.
Provides theoretical context for understanding the boundary between fine art and decorative/functional art — the central question of this section.
Renaissance decorative arts including tapestry, ceramics, metalwork, and jewelry are relevant to the section's treatment of decorative art types.
Ancient Egyptian decorative arts including jewelry, ceramics, and metalwork are relevant to the section's treatment of decorative art types and materials.
Christian decorative arts including stained glass, metalwork, manuscript illumination, and enamelwork are relevant to multiple subsections of B and C.
Relevant to the section's treatment of handwriting systems and styles, calligraphy, and lettering (section C.4.a).
Directly relevant to the section's treatment of handwriting systems and styles, calligraphy, and lettering (section C.4.a).
Relevant to the section's treatment of automata — decorative mechanical objects (section C.5.c).
Classical decorative arts including pottery, coins, medals, gems, and metalwork are relevant to multiple subsections.
Etruscan decorative arts including pottery, jewelry, and metalwork are relevant to the section's treatment of decorative art types.
Relevant to the section's treatment of decorative arts across cultures, including body decoration (section C.1.c) and decorative traditions using organic materials.
Prehistoric decorative arts including pottery, body decoration, and jewelry are among the earliest functional design objects.
Relevant to the section's treatment of the design of exhibitions and displays (section C.4.e) and the integration of decorative design with landscape.
Relevant to the section's treatment of advertising art and design (section C.4.c) and the design of exhibitions and displays (section C.4.e).
Relevant to the section's treatment of decorative uses of botanical materials including bouquets, garlands, and wreaths (section B.10.a).
Modernist design philosophy transformed industrial and interior design, relevant to sections C.2 and C.3.
Avant-garde movements challenged the distinction between fine art and decorative/functional art, central to this section's concerns.
Futurist design extended to furniture, textiles, and industrial objects, relevant to the section's treatment of industrial design and interior design.
Confucian aesthetics shaped East Asian decorative arts including ceramics, calligraphy, and lacquerwork — art forms discussed in sections B.1, B.11.b, and C.4.a.
